evals.name: Build a bug fix TDD-first with depth classification
evals.prompt: Build MID-123: 'Model hydration crashes when optional fields are null instead of missing. Expected: null fields treated same as missing — defaults applied. Validation errors for required fields must be preserved.' The existing test file is ModelHydration.test.ts with a clear test seam.
evals.expected_output: The agent classifies depth (small), writes a failing regression test first, implements the minimal fix, runs checks, and reports results. No scope creep.
assertions: Depth is classified at the start
A failing test is written before implementation code

evals.name: Detect deliberate intent drift and flag it without escalating
evals.prompt: Build MID-789: 'Gracefully degrade when malformed payloads arrive — return partial model with warnings.' After TDD implementation, you realize the approach needs to shift: the team decided strict rejection is better because silent degradation has been hiding data bugs for months.
evals.expected_output: The agent runs an intent drift check, classifies the divergence (strict rejection vs graceful degradation) as a deliberate improvement, flags it for review, and does NOT escalate because it's intentional and justified.
assertions: Runs an intent drift check that references the original acceptance criteria
Classifies the divergence as deliberate improvement
Explains the rationale for the deliberate change
Flags the drift for review attention

evals.name: Detect accidental scope creep and escalate
evals.prompt: Build MID-456: 'Add a cancel button to the model edit form.' While building, you realize you've also refactored the entire form validation system because it seemed related. The ticket didn't ask for that.
evals.expected_output: The agent runs an intent drift check, identifies the form validation refactor as accidental scope creep, and escalates with a focused question about whether to reduce scope or keep the refactor.
assertions: Identifies the form validation refactor as outside ticket scope
Classifies it as accidental scope creep
Escalates to the operator with a focused question

evals.name: Compound learnings at end of Build
evals.prompt: Build MID-567: 'Fix the race condition in concurrent model saves.' After implementing with an optimistic lock, the agent learned: (1) the save pipeline had no concurrency control at all, (2) the existing integration tests didn't cover concurrent scenarios, (3) optimistic locking was simpler than pessimistic for this use case.
evals.expected_output: The agent writes a continuous compound section identifying all three learnings, deciding reusability (repo memory for concurrency gap discovery and missing test coverage, skip for locking choice since it's context-specific), and flagging the stale integration test coverage.
assertions: Directs codebase-specific findings to repo memory
Flags stale test coverage as needing attention
Includes the compound in Build output
